content.assessment: From an educational-psychology lens, the status quo preserves the current early (age-10) selection point, which sustains the reference-group dynamics documented in the big-fish-little-pond literature: able students placed in less-selective schools may develop stronger self-concept than equally able peers in elite tracks, while non-selected students face a stratified peer environment associated with differentiated aspiration. Because monitoring is passive and not linked to correction mechanisms, none of the psychological costs of early sorting (labeling, performance-orientation pressure, potential stereotype-relevant dynamics for stigmatized groups) are addressed. The proposal itself introduces no new psychological mechanism; it maintains exposure to existing ones indefinitely.
opportunities: Monitoring infrastructure could be repurposed to test psychological hypotheses (self-concept, motivation trajectories) if paired with future intervention.
strengths: Continued data collection could, in principle, make visible the self-concept and reference-group patterns associated with the existing selective structure, informing future decisions.
threats: Absent corrective action, documented reference-group effects on self-concept and aspiration are likely to persist unabated for the affected population.
Erosion of legitimacy noted in the proposal's own risk list could itself interact with motivation and trust in the schooling system, though this is outside the curated psychological evidence base.
weaknesses: Preserves early selection age known to interact with big-fish-little-pond dynamics and reference-group effects on aspiration for equally able students sorted into different tiers.
Does not address performance-orientation and fear-of-failure dynamics associated with high-stakes ranking/selection structures.
No mechanism to mitigate contested but plausible labeling/expectancy effects on students in lower tracks.
